title: Multivariable chain rule
description: Given two functions, find the derivative of their composition.

hints:
## Formula

The multivariable chain rule says that $\dfrac{dh}{dt} = \nabla f(g(t)) \cdot g'(t)$.

The $g'(t)$ part is how much a change in $t$ will cause the input to $f$ to move, and the $\nabla f(g(t))$ part is how much $f$ will change in response to this update to its input.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

## Applying the formula

We want to find $h'(\pi) = \nabla f(g(\pi)) \cdot g'(\pi)$.

$\begin{align}
&g(\pi) = (-1, 0) \\ \\
&g'(\pi) = (-\sin(\pi), \cos(\pi)) = (0, -1) \\ \\
&\nabla f = (4xy, 2x^2) \\ \\
&\nabla f(g(\pi)) = \nabla f(-1, 0) = (0, 2)
\end{align}$

Substituting:

$h'(\pi) = (0, 2) \cdot (0, -1) = -2$

## Answer

$h'(\pi) = -2$

## Formula

The multivariable chain rule says that $\dfrac{dh}{dt} = \nabla f(g(t)) \cdot g'(t)$.

The $g'(t)$ part is how much a change in $t$ will cause the input to $f$ to move, and the $\nabla f(g(t))$ part is how much $f$ will change in response to this update to its input.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

## Applying the formula

We want to find $h'(4) = \nabla f(g(4)) \cdot g'(4)$.

$\begin{align}
&g(4) = (16, 8) \\ \\
&g'(4) = (2t, 2) = (8, 2) \\ \\
&\nabla f = (2, 3) \\ \\
&\nabla f(g(4)) = \nabla f(16, 8) = (2, 3)
\end{align}$

Substituting:

$h'(4) = (2, 3) \cdot (8, 2) = 16 + 6 = 22$

## Formula

The multivariable chain rule says that $\dfrac{dh}{dt} = \nabla f(g(t)) \cdot g'(t)$.

The $g'(t)$ part is how much a change in $t$ will cause the input to $f$ to move, and the $\nabla f(g(t))$ part is how much $f$ will change in response to this update to its input.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

## Applying the formula

We want to find $h'\left( \dfrac{\pi}{3} \right) = \nabla f(g(4)) \cdot g'(4)$.

$\begin{align}
&g\left( \dfrac{\pi}{3} \right) = \left( \dfrac{-2\pi}{3}, \dfrac{4\pi}{3} \right) \\ \\
&g'\left( \dfrac{\pi}{3} \right) = (-2, 4) \\ \\
&\nabla f = (-\cos(x), -\sin(y)) \\ \\
&\nabla f\left(g \left( \dfrac{\pi}{3} \right) \right) = \nabla f \left( \dfrac{-2\pi}{3}, \dfrac{4\pi}{3} \right) = \left( \dfrac{1}{2}, \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \right)
\end{align}$

Substituting:

$\begin{align}
h'\left( \dfrac{\pi}{3} \right) &= \left( \dfrac{1}{2}, \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \right) \cdot (-2, 4) \\ \\
&= -1 + 2\sqrt{3}
\end{align}$

## Answer

$h'\left( \dfrac{\pi}{3} \right) = -1 + 2\sqrt{3}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Let $f(x, y, z) = y^2z$ and $g(t) = (t, t^2, t^3)$.

$h(t) = f(g(t))$

$h'(2) = $ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
## Formula

The multivariable chain rule says that $\dfrac{dh}{dt} = \nabla f(g(t)) \cdot g'(t)$.

The $g'(t)$ part is how much a change in $t$ will cause the input to $f$ to move, and the $\nabla f(g(t))$ part is how much $f$ will change in response to this update to its input.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

## Applying the formula

We want to find $h'(2) = \nabla f(g(2)) \cdot g'(2)$.

$\begin{align}
&g(2) = (2, 4, 8) \\ \\
&g'(2) = (1, 2(2), 3(2)^2) = (1, 4, 12) \\ \\
&\nabla f = (0, 2yz, y^2) \\ \\
&\nabla f(g(2)) = \nabla f(2, 4, 8) = (0, 64, 16)
\end{align}$

Substituting:

$\begin{align}
h'(2) &= (0, 64, 16) \cdot (1, 4, 12) \\ \\
&= 256 + 192 \\ \\
&= 448
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Let $f(x, y, z) = xy - x\sqrt{z}$ and $g(t) = (2, -t, 4t^2)$.

$h(t) = f(g(t))$

$h'(1) = $ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
## Formula

The multivariable chain rule says that $\dfrac{dh}{dt} = \nabla f(g(t)) \cdot g'(t)$.

The $g'(t)$ part is how much a change in $t$ will cause the input to $f$ to move, and the $\nabla f(g(t))$ part is how much $f$ will change in response to this update to its input.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

## Applying the formula

We want to find $h'(1) = \nabla f(g(1)) \cdot g'(1)$.

$\begin{align}
&g(1) = (2, -1, 4) \\ \\
&g'(1) = (0, -1, 8t) = (0, -1, 8) \\ \\
&\nabla f = \left( y - \sqrt{z}, x, \dfrac{-x}{2\sqrt{z}} \right) \\ \\
&\nabla f(g(1)) = \nabla f(2, -1, 4) = \left( -3, 2, \dfrac{-1}{2} \right)
\end{align}$

Substituting:

$\begin{align}
h'(1) &= \left( -3, 2, \dfrac{-1}{2} \right) \cdot (0, -1, 8) \\ \\
&= -2 - 4 \\ \\
&= -6
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Let $f(x, y, z) = z - 3x^2 + 2y^2$ and $g(t) = (2t, \sqrt{2t}, t)$.

$h(t) = f(g(t))$

$h'(2) = $ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
## Formula

The multivariable chain rule says that $\dfrac{dh}{dt} = \nabla f(g(t)) \cdot g'(t)$.

The $g'(t)$ part is how much a change in $t$ will cause the input to $f$ to move, and the $\nabla f(g(t))$ part is how much $f$ will change in response to this update to its input.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

## Applying the formula

We want to find $h'(2) = \nabla f(g(2)) \cdot g'(2)$.

$\begin{align}
&g(2) = (4, 2, 2) \\ \\
&g'(2) = \left( 2, \dfrac{2}{2\sqrt{2t}}, 1 \right) = \left( 2, \dfrac{1}{2}, 1 \right) \\ \\
&\nabla f = \left( -6x, 4y, 1 \right) \\ \\
&\nabla f(g(2)) = \nabla f(4, 2, 2) = \left( -24, 8, 1 \right)
\end{align}$

Substituting:

$\begin{align}
h'(2) &= (-24, 8, 1) \cdot \left( 2, \dfrac{1}{2}, 1 \right) \\ \\
&= -48 + 4 + 1 \\ \\
&= -43
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Let $f(x, y) = \ln(x^2) + y$ and $g(t) = (\sin(t), -\cos(t))$.

$h(t) = f(g(t))$

$h'(t) = $ [[☃ expression 1]]
hints:
## Formula

The multivariable chain rule says that $\dfrac{dh}{dt} = \nabla f(g(t)) \cdot g'(t)$.

The $g'(t)$ part is how much a change in $t$ will cause the input to $f$ to move, and the $\nabla f(g(t))$ part is how much $f$ will change in response to this update to its input.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

## Applying the formula

We want to find $h'(t) = \nabla f(g(t)) \cdot g'(t)$.

$\begin{align}
&g(t) = (\sin(t), -\cos(t)) \\ \\
&g'(t) = (\cos(t), \sin(t)) \\ \\
&\nabla f = \left( 2x \dfrac{1}{x^2}, 1 \right) = \left( \dfrac{2}{x}, 1 \right) \\ \\
&\nabla f(g(t)) = \left( \dfrac{2}{\sin(t)}, 1 \right)
\end{align}$

Substituting:

$\begin{align}
h'(t) &= \left( \dfrac{2}{\sin(t)}, 1 \right) \cdot (\cos(t), \sin(t)) \\ \\
&= 2 \dfrac{\cos(t)}{\sin(t)} + \sin(t)
\end{align}$

## Answer

$h'(t) = 2 \dfrac{\cos(t)}{\sin(t)} + \sin(t)$

assessment_items.question_content:
Let $f(x, y) = e^{x^2 + y^2}$ and $g(t) = (4t, 3t)$.

$h(t) = f(g(t))$

$h'(t) = $ [[☃ expression 1]]
hints:
## Formula

The multivariable chain rule says that $\dfrac{dh}{dt} = \nabla f(g(t)) \cdot g'(t)$.

The $g'(t)$ part is how much a change in $t$ will cause the input to $f$ to move, and the $\nabla f(g(t))$ part is how much $f$ will change in response to this update to its input.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

## Applying the formula

We want to find $h'(t) = \nabla f(g(t)) \cdot g'(t)$.

$\begin{align}
&g(t) = (4t, 3t) \\ \\
&g'(t) = (4, 3) \\ \\
&\nabla f = \left( 2xe^{x^2 + y^2}, 2ye^{x^2 + y^2} \right) \\ \\
&\nabla f(g(t)) = \left( 8te^{25t^2}, 6te^{25t^2} \right)
\end{align}$

Substituting:

$\begin{align}
h'(t) &= \left( 8te^{25t^2}, 6te^{25t^2} \right) \cdot (4, 3) \\ \\
&= 32te^{25t^2} + 18te^{25t^2} \\ \\
&= 50te^{25t^2}
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Let $f(x, y) = \dfrac{x + y}{y - x}$ and $g(t) = (2 + t, 3 - t)$.

$h(t) = f(g(t))$

$h'(t) = $ [[☃ expression 1]]
hints:
## Formula

The multivariable chain rule says that $\dfrac{dh}{dt} = \nabla f(g(t)) \cdot g'(t)$.

The $g'(t)$ part is how much a change in $t$ will cause the input to $f$ to move, and the $\nabla f(g(t))$ part is how much $f$ will change in response to this update to its input.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

## Applying the formula

We want to find $h'(t) = \nabla f(g(t)) \cdot g'(t)$.

$\begin{align}
&g(t) = (2 + t, 3 - t) \\ \\
&g'(t) = (1, -1) \\ \\
&\nabla f = \left( \dfrac{2y}{(y - x)^2}, \dfrac{-2x}{(y - x)^2} \right) \\ \\
&\nabla f(g(t)) = \left( \dfrac{6 - 2t}{(1 - 2t)^2}, \dfrac{-4 - 2t}{(1 - 2t)^2} \right)
\end{align}$

Substituting:

$\begin{align}
h'(t) &= \left( \dfrac{6 - 2t}{(1 - 2t)^2}, \dfrac{-4 - 2t}{(1 - 2t)^2} \right) \cdot (1, -1) \\ \\
&= \dfrac{6 - 2t}{(1 - 2t)^2} + \dfrac{4 + 2t}{(1 - 2t)^2} \\ \\
&= \dfrac{10}{(1 - 2t)^2}
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Let $f(x, y, z) = xz + yz$ and $g(t) = (\cos(t), \sin(t), t)$.

$h(t) = f(g(t))$

$h'(t) = $ [[☃ expression 1]]
hints:
## Formula

The multivariable chain rule says that $\dfrac{dh}{dt} = \nabla f(g(t)) \cdot g'(t)$.

The $g'(t)$ part is how much a change in $t$ will cause the input to $f$ to move, and the $\nabla f(g(t))$ part is how much $f$ will change in response to this update to its input.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

## Applying the formula

We want to find $h'(t) = \nabla f(g(t)) \cdot g'(t)$.

$\begin{align}
&g(t) = (\cos(t), \sin(t), t) \\ \\
&g'(t) = (-\sin(t), \cos(t), 1) \\ \\
&\nabla f = (z, z, x + y) \\ \\
&\nabla f(g(t)) = \left( t, t, \cos(t) + \sin(t) \right)
\end{align}$

Substituting:

$h'(t) = -t \sin(t) + t \cos(t) + \cos(t) + \sin(t)$

## Answer

$h'(t) = -t \sin(t) + t \cos(t) + \cos(t) + \sin(t)$

assessment_items.question_content:
Let $f(x, y, z) = z\cos(y) + z^2x$ and $g(t) = (t, -t^2, -t)$.

$h(t) = f(g(t))$

$h'(t) = $ [[☃ expression 1]]
hints:
## Formula

The multivariable chain rule says that $\dfrac{dh}{dt} = \nabla f(g(t)) \cdot g'(t)$.

The $g'(t)$ part is how much a change in $t$ will cause the input to $f$ to move, and the $\nabla f(g(t))$ part is how much $f$ will change in response to this update to its input.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

## Applying the formula

We want to find $h'(t) = \nabla f(g(t)) \cdot g'(t)$.

$\begin{align}
&g(t) = (t, -t^2, -t) \\ \\
&g'(t) = (1, -2t, -1) \\ \\
&\nabla f = (z^2, -z\sin(y), \cos(y) + 2zx) \\ \\
&\nabla f(g(t)) = \left( t^2, t\sin(-t^2), \cos(-t^2) - 2t^2 \right)
\end{align}$

Substituting:

$\begin{align}
h'(t) &= t^2 - 2t^2\sin(-t^2) -\cos(-t^2) + 2t^2 \\ \\
&= 3t^2 + 2t^2\sin(t^2) - \cos(t^2)
\end{align}$

## Answer

$h'(t) = 3t^2 + 2t^2\sin(t^2) - \cos(t^2)$

assessment_items.question_content:
Let $f(x, y, z) = zx^2 + y^3$ and $g(t) = (t, \sin(3t), \sin(2t))$.

$h(t) = f(g(t))$

$h'(t) = $ [[☃ expression 1]]
hints:
## Formula

The multivariable chain rule says that $\dfrac{dh}{dt} = \nabla f(g(t)) \cdot g'(t)$.

The $g'(t)$ part is how much a change in $t$ will cause the input to $f$ to move, and the $\nabla f(g(t))$ part is how much $f$ will change in response to this update to its input.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

## Applying the formula

We want to find $h'(t) = \nabla f(g(t)) \cdot g'(t)$.

$\begin{align}
&g(t) = (t, \sin(3t), \sin(2t)) \\ \\
&g'(t) = (1, 3\cos(3t), 2\cos(2t)) \\ \\
&\nabla f = (2xz, 3y^2, x^2) \\ \\
&\nabla f(g(t)) = \left( 2t\sin(2t), 3\sin^2(3t), t^2 \right)
\end{align}$

Substituting:

$\begin{align}
h'(t) &= 2t\sin(2t) + 9\cos(3t)\sin^2(3t) + 2t^2\cos(2t)
\end{align}$

## Answer

$h'(t) = 2t\sin(2t) + 9\cos(3t)\sin^2(3t) + 2t^2\cos(2t)$
